model_summary: Initial gVAR-ready observation model focusing on situational cognitive-behavioral-emotional factors described in the complaint: attentional disengagement during conversations (C01), lapses in registering/recalling conversational content (C03), post-lapse negative self-evaluation (C04), and reorientation/repair of understanding (C06). Modifiable predictors emphasize sleep/circadian health (P01), attention regulation practice (P02), use of external cognitive supports (P03), distraction-reduced social environments (P04), and daytime physical activity/hydration (P05). This setup provides non-diagnostic, within-person variability-rich data to support subsequent gVAR modeling while balancing brevity and interpretability.
variable_selection_notes: Rationale: The complaint maps cleanly to four core, substantially distinct yet interrelated processes (attention during social exchange, immediate comprehension/recall, affective appraisal after lapses, and repair capacity). This reduces redundancy from multiple near-duplicate self-regulation constructs while preserving mechanism coverage across cognitive, affective, and behavioral domains. Predictors are chosen for measurable, safe, and modifiable influence on the core criteria; sleep and physical activity are known to modulate attention and affect, while explicit cognitive supports and environmental distraction controls offer actionable levers. Variance considerations: Likely within-person fluctuations are expected on 6-time-daily EMA prompts for momentary predictors/criteria, plus a single nightly diary for sleep-related predictors. Potential biases are mitigated by centering within-person and employing z-score transformations in analyses; monitor missingness and use imputation if needed. Trade-offs: a small, high-signal set reduces collinearity risk and improves estimability for early gVAR iterations, at the cost of potentially missing more granular subcomponents of self-regulation; planned re-selection after initial pilot will address this.

momentary_ema.notes: Distribute six prompts across four blocks with minor jitter to reduce predictability and promote coverage across morning/afternoon/evening.

daily_diary.notes: Evening diary capturing sleep quality and daily activity context.

passive_streams.notes: Not used in this initial plan to minimize data processing complexity.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Initial variable set is designed for straightforward gVAR estimation with 4 criteria and 5 predictors; low collinearity expected due to broad constructs and careful selection.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Center within-person data, apply z-scoring for predictors and criterion scores, consider log1p or other bounded transformations if skew emerges; expect high within-person variance given daily life fluctuations.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Prompts fatigue risk; implement reminders and a brief catch-up window; if prompts are missed, use last-observation-carried-forward imputation for short gaps; ensure diary entries maintain daily completion.

safety_notes: This is not a diagnosis or treatment guidance. If any emerging safety risk is detected during collection (e.g., self-harm ideation, severe distress), follow your standard safety protocol and contact appropriate professionals.

criteria_variables.label: Attentional disengagement during conversations

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, how distracted or mind-wandering are you during the current conversation? Rate from 1 (completely focused) to 9 (completely distracted).

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / bipolar and related disorders / unspecified bipolar and related disorder / distractibility
criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / bipolar and related disorders / unspecified bipolar and related disorder / *distractibility
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable: C01 Attentional disengagement during conversations; measurement: EMA_self_report via smartphone prompts asking how distracted or mind-wandering the respondent is during the current conversation, on a 9-point Likert scale. This maps directly to a 'distractibility' operational leaf, capturing momentary social-context attentional lapses and mind-wandering during conversations. Among candidates, 'distractibility' is the most specific and closest match to the intended variable (attentional disengagement in social exchange) compared to broader attention subtypes (sustained/selective attention) or social engagement constructs (engaged listening). This aligns with the pseudoprofile’s focus on within-person, time-varying attention during social interaction and its downstream mechanism (lapses, post-lapse appraisal, and repair).

criteria_variables.label: Failure to register/recall key conversational content

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, how well did you understand and register what was said in the most recent part of the conversation? Rate from 1 (fully understood) to 9 (not understood).

criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: The variable 'Failure to register/recall key conversational content' aligns best with the leaf 'recall_abilities' (memory skills). The measurement item asks how well you understood and registered the most recent part of the conversation, which maps to momentary recall/recall abilities rather than a broad clinical memory impairment. Other candidates describe specific clinical disorders or language comprehension deficits, which are less aligned with the intended within-person, non-diagnostic EMA context. Therefore, recall_abilities provides the most specific operational construct for this variable.

criteria_variables.label: Negative self-evaluation after lapses

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how much did you judge yourself negatively for attentional lapses? Rate from 1 (not at all) to 9 (very strongly).

criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable C04 maps to post-lapse negative self-evaluation (self-criticism after attentional lapses). The EMA item explicitly measures negative self-judgment following lapses, aligning with self-critical appraisal. The closest, most specific leaf is 'self_critical_rumination' (idx 19031), which operationalizes post-event rumination with a focus on self-criticism within an emotional/self-regulatory process framework. Although cataloged under a clinical path, this leaf serves as a non-diagnostic, mechanism-focused mapping for within-person EMA data in this context.

criteria_variables.label: Ability to reorient attention and repair understanding

measurement.item_or_signal: Capacity to notice the lapse, refocus on the speaker, and recover or clarify missed information (e.g., ask for repetition, summarize aloud). Rate from 1 (not capable) to 9 (highly capable).

criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable C06 targets the capacity to notice a lapse, redirect attention to the speaker, and recover or clarify information. The closest operational counterpart is the leaf 'self_repair_within_utterances' (idx=28171), which explicitly denotes self-repair within conversational utterances (e.g., asking for repetition, summarizing) and aligns with the EMA self-report measurement of repair ability. Among candidates, this leaf most precisely captures the repair/reorientation mechanism described by C06, whereas other leaves emphasize general attention or recall rather than reparative conversational repair.

Improved sleep supports comprehension/recall in conversations.
Sleep improves mood regulation, reducing self-criticism after lapses.
Rested state facilitates rapid reorientation and repair.

Training improves moment-to-moment comprehension.
Improved self-regulation lowers negative self-evaluation after lapses.
Enhanced regulation supports repair after lapses.

feasibility.notes: Feasible with 6 prompts/day; requires brief practice entry.

expected_collinearity_risks: Low expected collinearity among C01–C04–C06 with the five predictors due to broad domains.
note: possible minor overlap between C01 and C03 as attention and comprehension co-vary.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: Possible ceiling effects on 9-point scales if participants are highly engaged; monitor distributions and apply bounded transforms if needed.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Within-person centering (person-mean centering) of predictors and criteria.
Consider cubic or interaction terms if pilot indicates nonlinearity.
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: C02 (Automatic social responding despite inattention) omitted to reduce redundancy with C01 and to preserve model parsimony in initial iteration.
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: Feasible with 4 criteria and 5 predictors; momentary EMA (6 prompts/day) provides adequate within-person variance; nightly sleep diary adds sleep dimension; watch missingness and ensure reminders.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, how distracted or mind-wandering are you during the current conversation? Rate from 1 (completely focused) to 9 (completely distracted).

criteria.label: Failure to register/recall key conversational content
criteria.query_text_used: Failure to register/recall key conversational content | Operational item/signal: Right now, how well did you understand and register what was said in the most recent part of the conversation? Rate from 1 (fully understood) to 9 (not understood). | Polarity: unknown | Notes: In-the-moment comprehension.

mapping.rationale: The variable 'Failure to register/recall key conversational content' aligns best with the leaf 'recall_abilities' (memory skills). The measurement item asks how well you understood and registered the most recent part of the conversation, which maps to momentary recall/recall abilities rather than a broad clinical memory impairment. Other candidates describe specific clinical disorders or language comprehension deficits, which are less aligned with the intended within-person, non-diagnostic EMA context. Therefore, recall_abilities provides the most specific operational construct for this variable.

source_variable.label: Failure to register/recall key conversational content

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, how well did you understand and register what was said in the most recent part of the conversation? Rate from 1 (fully understood) to 9 (not understood).

criteria.label: Negative self-evaluation after lapses
criteria.query_text_used: Negative self-evaluation after lapses | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how much did you judge yourself negatively for attentional lapses? Rate from 1 (not at all) to 9 (very strongly). | Polarity: unknown | Notes: Self-criticism after lapses.

mapping.rationale: Variable C04 maps to post-lapse negative self-evaluation (self-criticism after attentional lapses). The EMA item explicitly measures negative self-judgment following lapses, aligning with self-critical appraisal. The closest, most specific leaf is 'self_critical_rumination' (idx 19031), which operationalizes post-event rumination with a focus on self-criticism within an emotional/self-regulatory process framework. Although cataloged under a clinical path, this leaf serves as a non-diagnostic, mechanism-focused mapping for within-person EMA data in this context.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how much did you judge yourself negatively for attentional lapses? Rate from 1 (not at all) to 9 (very strongly).

criteria.label: Ability to reorient attention and repair understanding
criteria.query_text_used: Ability to reorient attention and repair understanding | Operational item/signal: Capacity to notice the lapse, refocus on the speaker, and recover or clarify missed information (e.g., ask for repetition, summarize aloud). Rate from 1 (not capable) to 9 (highly capable). | Polarity: unknown | Notes: Repair after lapses.

mapping.rationale: Variable C06 targets the capacity to notice a lapse, redirect attention to the speaker, and recover or clarify information. The closest operational counterpart is the leaf 'self_repair_within_utterances' (idx=28171), which explicitly denotes self-repair within conversational utterances (e.g., asking for repetition, summarizing) and aligns with the EMA self-report measurement of repair ability. Among candidates, this leaf most precisely captures the repair/reorientation mechanism described by C06, whereas other leaves emphasize general attention or recall rather than reparative conversational repair.

source_variable.label: Ability to reorient attention and repair understanding

measurement.item_or_signal: Capacity to notice the lapse, refocus on the speaker, and recover or clarify missed information (e.g., ask for repetition, summarize aloud). Rate from 1 (not capable) to 9 (highly capable).

predictors.label: Sleep quality and circadian alignment
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep quality and circadian alignment | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Adequate sleep supports attention and cognitive processing. | Intervention examples: maintain regular sleep-wake times; limit late caffeine | Measurement: EMA_self_report | How well did you sleep last night? Rate 1 (very poor) to 9 (excellent). | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] > Sleep_and_Circadian > Sleep_Routines_and_Practices

mapping.rationale: Variable maps to Sleep quality and circadian alignment (BIO domain). The best-matching leaf is 4548: BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / self_assessment_and_logs / daily_and_retrospective_logs / sleep_quality_rating, i.e., a sleep quality rating (sleep_quality_rating) captured as a morning diary item (EMA_self_report, 9-point scale). Mechanistically, sleep quality influences daytime attention and cognitive processing, aligning with the P01 predictor's focus on sleep/circadian health. The item matches the variable label (Sleep quality and circadian alignment) and the specified measurement: 9-point Likert, one observation per day via morning diary.

source_variable.label: Sleep quality and circadian alignment
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Adequate sleep supports attention and cognitive processing.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: maintain regular sleep-wake times; limit late caffeine

measurement.item_or_signal: How well did you sleep last night? Rate 1 (very poor) to 9 (excellent).

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep is expected to reduce in-conversation distraction.
Improved sleep supports comprehension/recall in conversations.
Sleep improves mood regulation, reducing self-criticism after lapses.
Rested state facilitates rapid reorientation and repair.

predictors.label: Attention regulation practice
predictors.query_text_used: Attention regulation practice | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Regular practice strengthens attentional control. | Intervention examples: daily 5–10 minute focus/attention drills | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today, I completed a 5–10 minute attention/awareness practice. On a scale 1-9, how true is this for you right now? (1 = not at all, 9 = completely). | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Cognitive_Capacity_Enhancement_and_Stimulation > Attention_Systems_Training

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: PSYCHO / third_wave_and_contextual_therapies / mindfulness_based_interventions / core_attentional_skills / sustained_attention_practice. The variable label 'Attention regulation practice' aligns with 'sustained_attention_practice' as the operational construct. The measurement item explicitly asks about completing a 5–10 minute attention/awareness practice (EMA self-report), which directly corresponds to sustained attention practice. Mechanism rationale matches: regular practice strengthens attentional control. This mapping aligns with the pseudoprofile's focus on attention regulation as a modifiable predictor (P02) within within-person EMA data.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, I completed a 5–10 minute attention/awareness practice. On a scale 1-9, how true is this for you right now? (1 = not at all, 9 = completely).

expected_effects.rationale: More practice reduces in-conversation disengagement.
Training improves moment-to-moment comprehension.
Improved self-regulation lowers negative self-evaluation after lapses.
Enhanced regulation supports repair after lapses.

feasibility.notes: Feasible with 6 prompts/day; requires brief practice entry.

predictors.label: External cognitive supports during conversations
predictors.query_text_used: External cognitive supports during conversations | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Notes/prompts reduce cognitive load, aiding comprehension and recall. | Intervention examples: keep a compact notes prompt within reach | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In this moment, are you using notes or prompts to help you understand or remember what's being said? Rate 1-9. | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Digital_and_Tech_Enabled_Solutions > Care_Delivery_Formats

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: idx 2917 ('ema_prompting_tool'). This predictor directly corresponds to External cognitive supports during conversations by capturing use of notes/prompts as an EMA-based tool. Measurement item matches: 'In this moment, are you using notes or prompts to help you understand or remember what's being said?' on a 9-point Likert scale, with EMA_self_report as measurement mode and smartphone EMA channel. Mechanism rationalizes that notes/prompts reduce cognitive load and aid comprehension/recall, aligning with the intended external-cognitive-support construct. Ontology path aligns under PSYCHO / Digital_and_Tech_Enabled_Solutions / Digital_tools_and_supports / ema_prompting_tool.

source_variable.label: External cognitive supports during conversations
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Notes/prompts reduce cognitive load, aiding comprehension and recall.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: keep a compact notes prompt within reach

measurement.item_or_signal: In this moment, are you using notes or prompts to help you understand or remember what's being said? Rate 1-9.
measurement.notes: Use of external aids during conversations.

expected_effects.rationale: Supportive prompts reduce disengagement.
Cues improve uptake of content.
Perceived support reduces negative self-evaluation after lapses.
Aid in reorientation and repair.

feasibility.notes: Feasible with quick prompts; ensure accessibility of supports.

predictors.label: Distraction-minimized social environment during conversations
predictors.query_text_used: Distraction-minimized social environment during conversations | Domain: SOCIAL | Mechanism: Lower external distractions support sustained attention. | Intervention examples: phone on silent; quiet physical environment; background noise control | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In this moment, are you in a distraction-reduced setting during the conversation (e.g., phone on silent, quiet environment)? Rate 1-9. | Prior ontology hint: [SOCIAL] > Digital_Social_Environment > Digital_Tools_and_Supports

mapping.rationale: Best-matching PREDICTOR leaf for var_id P04 (Distraction-minimized social environment during conversations) is idx 76: SOCIAL / social_media_ecosystem / social_media_ecosystem_and_interventions / addiction_related_digital_use_and_supports / notification_and_interruption_control / quiet_mode_and_focus_mode_enablement_setup. This leaf directly targets reducing external distractions (notifications, interruptions) to support sustained attention in social conversations. The measurement item matches the provided EMA self-report question: 'In this moment, are you in a distraction-reduced setting during the conversation (e.g., phone on silent, quiet environment)?' Rated on a 9-point Likert, aligning with momentary assessment. Mechanistically, lowering external distractions enhances attentional focus during social exchanges, consistent with the variable’s mechanism rationale. The pseudoprofile context notes P04 as a modifiable predictor involving distraction-reduced environments, supporting this mapping as the most specific operational construct available.

source_variable.label: Distraction-minimized social environment during conversations
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Lower external distractions support sustained attention.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: phone on silent; quiet physical environment; background noise control

measurement.item_or_signal: In this moment, are you in a distraction-reduced setting during the conversation (e.g., phone on silent, quiet environment)? Rate 1-9.

expected_effects.rationale: Less distraction reduces disengagement in real time.
Less distraction supports comprehension in the moment.
Calm environments may reduce self-criticism after lapses.
Fewer distractions facilitate reorientation.

feasibility.notes: Moderate feasibility; ensure user comfort with environmental adjustments.

predictors.label: Aerobic exercise and hydration
predictors.query_text_used: Aerobic exercise and hydration | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Exercise and hydration boost brain function and alertness. | Intervention examples: daily walk; water intake goals | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today I engaged in aerobic exercise and stayed hydrated. Rate 1-9 (1 = not at all, 9 = fully). | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] > Movement_and_Bodywork > Aerobic_Movement_Modalities

mapping.rationale: Variable maps to aerobic exercise component; measurement item indicates Today I engaged in aerobic exercise and stayed hydrated. This aligns with a leaf under BIO / movement_physical_function_and_somatic_care / aerobic_and_cardiorespiratory_movement / water_based_aerobic / water_based_aerobic / *water_aerobics, the most specific operational construct among candidates for the exercise dimension. Hydration is a separate but related facet; given the requirement to select a single leaf, this leaf best captures the core predictor (aerobic activity) while acknowledging hydration is a related modulator. Mechanism rationale: exercise improves brain function and alertness; hydration supports cognitive performance, consistent with the variable’s mechanism_rationale.

source_variable.label: Aerobic exercise and hydration
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Exercise and hydration boost brain function and alertness.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today I engaged in aerobic exercise and stayed hydrated. Rate 1-9 (1 = not at all, 9 = fully).

expected_effects.rationale: Regular exercise relates to better attention and reduced disengagement.
Improved cognitive function supports recall.
Exercise-related mood stability reduces self-judgment after lapses.
Physical fitness facilitates reorientation and repair.

feasibility.notes: Daily diary acceptable; potential missing days due to schedule.
